docs.text: Description: Problems involving a grid (matrix) where you need to find a path or a property based on grid movement. - पहचान (Identification): - Movement restricted (e.g., only right and down). - Fixed or variable starting and ending points. - Grid with obstacles. - Example Problems: - Ninjas Training - Grid Unique Paths - Unique Paths 2 - Minimum Path Sum in Grid - Triangle DP - Cherry Pickup II

docs.title: 3. Subsequence DP
docs.text: Description: Problems where you pick or skip elements from an array to form a subsequence that satisfies a condition. - पहचान (Identification): - "Find a subset with sum K." - "Partition array into two subsets." - "Knapsack problems" (0/1 or Unbounded). - Uses the Pick/Non-Pick strategy. - Example Problems: - Subset Sum Equals Target - 0-1 Knapsack - Minimum Coins - Target Sum - Rod Cutting Problem

docs.title: 4. DP on Strings
docs.text: Description: Problems involving comparisons or operations on two or more strings. - पहचान (Identification): - "Longest Common Subsequence/Substring." - "Edit Distance" (Convert string A to B). - Palindromic properties within strings. - Wildcard or Regex matching. - Example Problems: - Longest Common Subsequence - Edit Distance - Longest Palindromic Subsequence - Distinct Subsequences - Wildcard Matching

docs.title: 5. Stock DP
docs.text: Description: Problems focused on maximizing profit from buying and selling stocks under specific constraints. - पहचान (Identification): - "Maximum profit with K transactions." - "Unlimited transactions but cannot hold more than one stock." - "Transaction fees" or "Cooldown periods." - Example Problems: - Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock - Buy and Sell Stock II - Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown - Buy and Sell Stock with Transaction Fee

docs.title: 6. LIS (Longest Increasing Subsequence) Type
docs.text: Description: Problems centered around finding the longest subsequence that follows an increasing or specific order. - पहचान (Identification): - Elements must follow an order: \(arr[i] > arr[prev\_index]\). - "Largest Divisible Subset." - "Longest String Chain." - "Bitonic Subsequences." - Example Problems: - Longest Increasing Subsequence - Largest Divisible Subset - Longest String Chain - Longest Bitonic Subsequence

docs.title: 7. Partition DP (MCM Type)
docs.text: Description: Problems where the array is partitioned at various points, and the solution is built from these partitions. - पहचान (Identification): - Problems involving optimal "cutting" or "splitting." - "Matrix Chain Multiplication." - Result depends on the order of operations. - Example Problems: - Matrix Chain Multiplication - Minimum Cost to Cut the Stick - Burst Balloons - Palindrome Partitioning II

docs.title: 8. DP on Rectangles
docs.text: Description: Problems dealing with subgrids or rectangles within a larger matrix. - पहचान (Identification): - "Maximum area rectangle with all 1s." - "Count square submatrices." - Example Problems: - Maximum Rectangle Area with all 1s - Count Square Submatrices with all Ones

docs.title: Dynamic Programming Templates (Python)
docs.text: Standard templates to solve DP problems in Python. These templates follow a consistent structure for different optimization levels.

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization (Top-Down)
docs.text:
This is the most intuitive approach. We use a dictionary or an array to store results of subproblems.

from functools import lru_cache

def solve():
    arr = [1, 2, 3, 4]
    n = len(arr)

    # Using lru_cache for automatic memoization
    @lru_cache(None)
    def f(ind):
        # 1. Base Case
        if ind == 0:
            return arr[0]
        if ind < 0:
            return 0

        # 2. Recursive calls (Pick / Non-Pick or state transitions)
        pick = arr[ind] + f(ind - 2)
        not_pick = 0 + f(ind - 1)

        # 3. Return result
        return max(pick, not_pick)

    return f(n - 1)

# Manual Memoization Template
def solve_manual():
    arr = [1, 2, 3, 4]
    n = len(arr)
    dp = [-1] * n

    def f(ind):
        if ind == 0: return arr[0]
        if ind < 0: return 0

        if dp[ind] != -1: return dp[ind]

        pick = arr[ind] + f(ind - 2)
        not_pick = 0 + f(ind - 1)

        dp[ind] = max(pick, not_pick)
        return dp[ind]

    return f(n - 1)

docs.title: 2. Tabulation (Bottom-Up)
docs.text:
Iterative approach that fills a DP table. Useful for avoiding recursion depth limits.

def solve_tabulation():
    arr = [1, 2, 3, 4]
    n = len(arr)
    dp = [0] * n

    # 1. Initialize Base Case
    dp[0] = arr[0]

    # 2. Iterate through states
    for i in range(1, n):
        pick = arr[i]
        if i > 1:
            pick += dp[i - 2]

        not_pick = 0 + dp[i - 1]

        # 3. Store result in table
        dp[i] = max(pick, not_pick)

    return dp[n - 1]

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization (1D DP)
docs.text:
Reduces space from \(O(N)\) to \(O(1)\) by only keeping track of required previous states.

def solve_space_optimized():
    arr = [1, 2, 3, 4]
    n = len(arr)

    prev = arr[0]      # dp[i-1]
    prev2 = 0         # dp[i-2]

    for i in range(1, n):
        pick = arr[i] + prev2
        not_pick = 0 + prev

        curi = max(pick, not_pick)

        # Move pointers forward
        prev2 = prev
        prev = curi

    return prev

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization (2D/Grid DP)
docs.text:
Reduces space from \(O(N \times M)\) to \(O(M)\) by using only the prev row.

def solve_grid_space_optimized(matrix):
    n = len(matrix)
    m = len(matrix[0])

    # Use a single row to store results of the previous row
    prev = [0] * m

    for i in range(n):
        temp = [0] * m
        for j in range(m):
            if i == 0 and j == 0:
                temp[j] = matrix[0][0]
            else:
                up = matrix[i][j]
                if i > 0: up += prev[j]
                else: up += float('inf') # Or suitable boundary value

                left = matrix[i][j]
                if j > 0: left += temp[j-1]
                else: left += float('inf')

                temp[j] = min(up, left)
        prev = temp

    return prev[m-1]

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: A thief wants to steal items from a house. There are \(N\) items, each with a weight and a value. The thief has a bag with a maximum capacity W. The goal is to maximize the total value of items in the bag without exceeding the capacity.
  • Constraints: 0/1 means you can either take an item (1) or leave it (0). You cannot take fractional items.
  • Input: Weights array wt, Values array val, number of items n, and capacity W.
  • Output: Maximum value.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• Pick/Not-Pick: For every item, the thief has two choices:
  • Not Pick: The value is the same as what could be obtained from the remaining items with the same bag capacity.
  • Pick: If the item's weight is less than or equal to the current capacity, add its value to the result of picking from remaining items with reduced capacity.
  • Recursive Relation: f(index, capacity) = max(not_pick, pick).

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use a 2D dp table of size n x (W + 1).
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(index, capacity, wt, val, dp):
    if index == 0:
        if wt[0] <= capacity:
            return val[0]
        return 0

    if dp[index][capacity] != -1:
        return dp[index][capacity]

    not_pick = solve(index - 1, capacity, wt, val, dp)
    pick = -1e9
    if wt[index] <= capacity:
        pick = val[index] + solve(index - 1, capacity - wt[index], wt, val, dp)

    dp[index][capacity] = max(pick, not_pick)
    return dp[index][capacity]

docs.title: 2. Tabulation
docs.text:
• Explanation: Build the table bottom-up from base cases.
  • Code (Python): 
    def knapsack(n, w, wt, val):
    dp = [[0 for _ in range(w + 1)] for _ in range(n)]

    # Base case: for index 0, if weight <= capacity, we take it
    for capacity in range(wt[0], w + 1):
        dp[0][capacity] = val[0]

    for i in range(1, n):
        for capacity in range(w + 1):
            not_pick = dp[i-1][capacity]
            pick = -1e9
            if wt[i] <= capacity:
                pick = val[i] + dp[i-1][capacity - wt[i]]
            dp[i][capacity] = max(pick, not_pick)

    return dp[n-1][w]

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization (Two Rows)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use prev and cur arrays.
  • Code (Python): 
    def knapsack(n, w, wt, val):
    prev = [0] * (w + 1)
    for capacity in range(wt[0], w + 1):
        prev[capacity] = val[0]

    for i in range(1, n):
        cur = [0] * (w + 1)
        for capacity in range(w + 1):
            not_pick = prev[capacity]
            pick = -1e9
            if wt[i] <= capacity:
                pick = val[i] + prev[capacity - wt[i]]
            cur[capacity] = max(pick, not_pick)
        prev = cur
    return prev[w]

docs.title: 4. Ultimate Space Optimization (Single Row)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Notice that when calculating cur[capacity], we only need values from prev at indices capacity and capacity - wt[i]. If we iterate capacity backwards from W to 0, we can update the same array without overwriting values needed for the same row.
  • Code (Python): 
    def knapsack(n, w, wt, val):
    prev = [0] * (w + 1)
    for capacity in range(wt[0], w + 1):
        prev[capacity] = val[0]

    for i in range(1, n):
        # Iterate backwards to use only one array
        for capacity in range(w, -1, -1):
            not_pick = prev[capacity]
            pick = -1e9
            if wt[i] <= capacity:
                pick = val[i] + prev[capacity - wt[i]]
            prev[capacity] = max(pick, not_pick)

    return prev[w]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][w] is the maximum value that can be obtained using items from index 0 to i with a bag capacity of w.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times W)\)
  • Space Complexity:
  • \(O(N \times W)\) for Memoization/Tabulation.
  • \(O(W)\) for Space Optimization (Single Row).

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Subsequences: The classic 0/1 Knapsack pattern.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Base Case: Forgetting that at index 0, you can take the item for all capacities \(\ge wt[0]\), not just exactly \(wt[0]\).
  • Backwards Iteration: In the single-row optimization, if you iterate forwards, you will accidentally use the updated value (representing "pick same item multiple times"), which converts the problem into Unbounded Knapsack.

docs.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the \(i^{th}\) day. You want to maximize your profit by choosing a single day to buy one stock and choosing a different day in the future to sell that stock.

docs.title: Input/Output
docs.text:
• Input: prices = [7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]
  • Output: 5 (Buy on day 2 at price 1, sell on day 5 at price 6)

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• \(1 \le prices.length \le 10^5\)
  • \(0 \le prices[i] \le 10^4\)

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text: To maximize profit with one transaction, we need to find the maximum difference between two prices, where the selling price occurs after the buying price. - For any day \(i\), if we decide to sell on this day, we should have bought on the day with the minimum price between day \(0\) and day \(i-1\). - We can maintain a running minimum of prices as we iterate through the array.

docs.title: 1. Greedy / 1D DP
docs.text: As we iterate through the prices: 1. Update the minPrice encountered so far. 2. Calculate the potential profit if we sell today: prices[i] - minPrice. 3. Update maxProfit if the current profit is higher.

docs.title: 2. Time and Space
docs.text: This approach only requires a single pass and a few variables.

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: While often categorized as Greedy, it can be seen as a simple DP where minPrice[i] is the minimum price from day \(0\) to \(i\). maxProfit = max(maxProfit, prices[i] - minPrice)

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text: minPrice = min(minPrice, prices[i]) profit = prices[i] - minPrice maxProfit = max(maxProfit, profit)

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity (Time & Space)
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N)\) - Single pass through the array.
  • Space: \(O(1)\) - Only a few variables (minPrice, maxProfit).

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: Stock DP (1 Transaction)

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Selling before buying: Ensure the logic always subtracts a previous minimum from the current price.
  • Negative Profit: If no profit is possible (prices only decrease), the answer should be 0.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: You are given n balloons, each with a number. If you burst balloon i, you get nums[i-1] * nums[i] * nums[i+1] coins. After bursting, the neighbors become adjacent. Find the maximum coins you can collect.
  • Constraints: If i-1 or i+1 is out of bounds, treat it as a balloon with value 1.
  • Example: nums = [3, 1, 5, 8]
  • Burst 1: 3*1*5 = 15. Remaining: [3, 5, 8]
  • Burst 5: 3*5*8 = 120. Remaining: [3, 8]
  • Burst 3: 1*3*8 = 24. Remaining: [8]
  • Burst 8: 1*8*1 = 8.
  • Total: 15 + 120 + 24 + 8 = 167.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• The Challenge: If we try to decide which balloon to burst first, the subproblems are dependent. Bursting a balloon changes the neighbors for the remaining balloons, making it impossible to solve left and right parts independently.
  • The Reverse Strategy: Instead of "which balloon to burst first", think "which balloon is the last to burst in the range [i, j]".
  • If balloon k is the last to burst in range [i, j]:
  • All balloons from i to k-1 and k+1 to j are already gone.
  • The neighbors of k at the moment of bursting will be nums[i-1] and nums[j+1].
  • Cost = nums[i-1] * nums[k] * nums[j+1] + f(i, k-1) + f(k+1, j).

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Add 1 to both ends of the array. i and j will represent the current range of balloons.
  • Code (C++): 
    int solve(int i, int j, vector<int>& nums, vector<vector<int>>& dp) {
    if (i > j) return 0;
    if (dp[i][j] != -1) return dp[i][j];

    int maxi = -1e9;
    for (int k = i; k <= j; k++) {
        int coins = nums[i - 1] * nums[k] * nums[j + 1] 
                    + solve(i, k - 1, nums, dp) 
                    + solve(k + 1, j, nums, dp);
        maxi = max(maxi, coins);
    }
    return dp[i][j] = maxi;
}

int maxCoins(vector<int>& nums) {
    int n = nums.size();
    nums.insert(nums.begin(), 1);
    nums.push_back(1);
    vector<vector<int>> dp(n + 1, vector<int>(n + 1, -1));
    return solve(1, n, nums, dp);
}

docs.title: 2. Tabulation
docs.text:
• Explanation: Iterate through the range length from 1 to n.
  • Code (C++): 
    int maxCoins(vector<int>& nums) {
    int n = nums.size();
    nums.insert(nums.begin(), 1);
    nums.push_back(1);
    vector<vector<int>> dp(n + 2, vector<int>(n + 2, 0));

    for (int i = n; i >= 1; i--) {
        for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
            if (i > j) continue;
            int maxi = -1e9;
            for (int k = i; k <= j; k++) {
                int coins = nums[i - 1] * nums[k] * nums[j + 1] 
                            + dp[i][k - 1] + dp[k + 1][j];
                maxi = max(maxi, coins);
            }
            dp[i][j] = maxi;
        }
    }
    return dp[1][n];
}

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j] is the maximum coins obtained by bursting all balloons in the range [i, j].

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N^3)\) (Two nested loops for i and j, and one internal loop for k).
  • Space Complexity: \(O(N^2)\) for the DP table.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • Partition DP (Bottom-Up Logic): Solving the "last step" first to ensure subproblem independence.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Top-Down Logic: Trying to pick the first balloon to burst. This fails because the subproblems remain dependent on neighbors outside their range that haven't been bursted yet.
  • Indices: Be careful with the i-1 and j+1 indices after padding the array with 1s.

docs.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the \(i^{th}\) day. You can perform unlimited transactions (buy and sell multiple times), but you must sell the stock before you can buy again.

docs.title: Input/Output
docs.text:
• Input: prices = [7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]
  • Output: 7 (Buy at 1, sell at 5 [profit 4]; buy at 3, sell at 6 [profit 3]; Total = 7)

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• \(1 \le prices.length \le 3 \times 10^4\)
  • \(0 \le prices[i] \le 10^4\)

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text: Since we can perform multiple transactions, we want to capture every price increase. - On any given day, we have two states: we either own a stock or we don't. - If we don't own a stock, we can either buy today or skip. - If we own a stock, we can either sell today or skip. This "Choice" leads to a recursive structure.

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text: Define f(ind, buy): - buy = 1: Can buy. Profit = max(-prices[ind] + f(ind+1, 0), f(ind+1, 1)) - buy = 0: Can sell. Profit = max(prices[ind] + f(ind+1, 1), f(ind+1, 0))

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text: Use a 2D array dp[n][2] to store results of f(ind, buy).

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text: Iterate from n-1 down to 0. Base case: dp[n][0] = dp[n][1] = 0.

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text: Since dp[ind] only depends on dp[ind+1], we can use two variables (or an array of size 2) for the "ahead" state and the "current" state.

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: dp[ind][buy] is the maximum profit we can make from day ind onwards, given our current status buy (1 if we can buy, 0 if we must sell).

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• Buying state: profit = max(-prices[ind] + dp[ind+1][0], 0 + dp[ind+1][1])
  • Selling state: profit = max(prices[ind] + dp[ind+1][1], 0 + dp[ind+1][0])

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity (Time & Space)
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N)\)
  • Space: \(O(1)\) with space optimization (or \(O(N)\) for memoization/tabulation).

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: Stock DP (Infinite Transactions)

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Overlapping Transactions: You cannot buy again if you already own a stock. The buy variable correctly tracks this.
  • Initialization: Forgetting to initialize the DP table with -1 (memoization).

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Transaction Fee

docs.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the \(i^{th}\) day. You can perform at most two transactions. You must sell the stock before you can buy again.

docs.title: Input/Output
docs.text:
• Input: prices = [3, 3, 5, 0, 0, 3, 1, 4]
  • Output: 6 (Buy at 0, sell at 3 [profit 3]; buy at 1, sell at 4 [profit 3]; Total = 6)

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• \(1 \le prices.length \le 10^5\)
  • \(0 \le prices[i] \le 10^5\)

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
This is an extension of the infinite transactions problem, but now we have a limit on the number of transactions. - A transaction is completed when a Buy followed by a Sell occurs. - We need a third state in our DP: cap, representing the number of transactions remaining. - When we sell, we decrease cap by 1.

Alternatively, we can think of it as 4 stages: Buy1, Sell1, Buy2, Sell2.

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text: Define f(ind, buy, cap): - buy = 1, cap > 0: max(-prices[ind] + f(ind+1, 0, cap), f(ind+1, 1, cap)) - buy = 0, cap > 0: max(prices[ind] + f(ind+1, 1, cap-1), f(ind+1, 0, cap)) - Base Case: if (ind == n || cap == 0) return 0;

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text: Use a 3D array dp[n][2][3] (2 for buy/sell, 3 for 0, 1, 2 remaining transactions).

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text: 3D table iterating ind from n-1 to 0, buy from 0 to 1, and cap from 1 to 2.

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text: Since dp[ind] only depends on dp[ind+1], we can reduce the 3D DP to two 2D arrays (after[2][3] and curr[2][3]).

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: dp[ind][buy][cap] is the maximum profit we can make from day ind onwards, given our current status buy and remaining transaction capacity cap.

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• If can buy: dp[i][1][cap] = max(-prices[i] + dp[i+1][0][cap], dp[i+1][1][cap])
  • If can sell: dp[i][0][cap] = max(prices[i] + dp[i+1][1][cap-1], dp[i+1][0][cap])

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity (Time & Space)
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N \times 2 \times 3) = O(N)\)
  • Space: \(O(1)\) with space optimization (fixed size array 2x3).

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: Stock DP (K Transactions)

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Transaction count: Only decrement the capacity when a full transaction (buy+sell) is completed. In the video's approach, cap is decremented at the Sell step.
  • Base cases: Forgetting to handle cap == 0.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IV (At most K transactions)
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II (Infinite transactions)

docs.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IV
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the \(i^{th}\) day, and an integer k. You can perform at most k transactions.

docs.title: Input/Output
docs.text:
• Input: k = 2, prices = [2, 4, 1]
  • Output: 2 (Buy at 2, sell at 4)

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• \(0 \le k \le 100\)
  • \(0 \le prices.length \le 1000\)

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text: This is the generalized version of Stock III. Instead of a fixed capacity of 2, we use a variable capacity k. The state can be represented in two ways: 1. (index, buy, capacity): where capacity goes from k down to 0. (3D DP) 2. (index, transactionNumber): where transactionNumber goes from 0 to 2*k - 1. (2D DP) - Even transactionNumber: Buy - Odd transactionNumber: Sell

docs.title: 1. Recursion (Capacity method)
docs.text: f(ind, buy, cap): - Similar to Stock III, but initialize cap = k.

docs.title: 2. Recursion (Transaction Number method)
docs.text: f(ind, tranNo): - If tranNo % 2 == 0 (Buy): max(-prices[ind] + f(ind+1, tranNo+1), f(ind+1, tranNo)) - If tranNo % 2 == 1 (Sell): max(prices[ind] + f(ind+1, tranNo+1), f(ind+1, tranNo))

docs.title: 3. Memoization
docs.text:
• Capacity method: dp[n][2][k+1]
  • Transaction method: dp[n][2*k]

docs.title: 4. Tabulation
docs.text: Convert the recursive calls to iterative loops. For the transaction method, the state dp[ind][tranNo] only depends on dp[ind+1].

docs.title: 5. Space Optimization
docs.text: Use two rows: after and curr. - Capacity method: after[2][k+1] - Transaction method: after[2*k + 1]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: Transaction Method: dp[ind][tranNo] is the maximum profit starting from day ind with tranNo transactions already initiated.

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
Transaction Method: 

if (tranNo % 2 == 0) // Buy
    dp[i][tranNo] = max(-prices[i] + dp[i+1][tranNo+1], dp[i+1][tranNo]);
else // Sell
    dp[i][tranNo] = max(prices[i] + dp[i+1][tranNo+1], dp[i+1][tranNo]);

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity (Time & Space)
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N \times K)\)
  • Space: \(O(K)\) with space optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: Stock DP (K Transactions)

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Capacity: If using the transaction number method, remember that k transactions mean 2*k stages (buy and sell).
  • Index Out of Bounds: When using tranNo + 1, ensure the DP table size is 2*k + 1.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Transaction Fee

docs.title: Buy and Sell Stocks with Cooldown
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: You are given an array of stock prices. You can buy and sell stocks unlimited times to maximize profit, but with a specific constraint: after selling a stock, you cannot buy again on the very next day. You must wait for at least one day (cooldown).
  • Goal: Maximize the total profit.
  • Constraints: After selling on day i, the next day you can buy is day i + 2.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This is a variation of the "Buy and Sell Stocks II" problem (unlimited transactions).
  • In Stock II, when we sell, we move to index + 1 and are allowed to buy.
  • In this problem, when we sell, we move to index + 2 to account for the 1-day cooldown.
  • For every day, we have two choices:
  • If we can buy: Either buy today or skip.
  • If we can sell: Either sell today or skip. If we sell, we must skip the next day (cooldown).

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use a 2D dp table of size n x 2. The index goes up to n, and buy is 0 or 1.
  • Code (C++): 
    int solve(int index, int buy, vector<int>& prices, vector<vector<int>>& dp) {
    if (index >= prices.size()) return 0;

    if (dp[index][buy] != -1) return dp[index][buy];

    if (buy) {
        // Buy or Skip
        return dp[index][buy] = max(-prices[index] + solve(index + 1, 0, prices, dp),
                                   0 + solve(index + 1, 1, prices, dp));
    } else {
        // Sell (move to index + 2) or Skip
        return dp[index][buy] = max(prices[index] + solve(index + 2, 1, prices, dp),
                                   0 + solve(index + 1, 0, prices, dp));
    }
}

docs.title: 2. Tabulation
docs.text:
• Explanation: Bottom-up approach. Note that since we access index + 2, the DP table should be of size n + 2.
  • Code (C++): 
    int stockCooldown(vector<int>& prices) {
    int n = prices.size();
    vector<vector<int>> dp(n + 2, vector<int>(2, 0));

    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        // Buy case
        dp[i][1] = max(-prices[i] + dp[i + 1][0], 0 + dp[i + 1][1]);
        // Sell case
        dp[i][0] = max(prices[i] + dp[i + 2][1], 0 + dp[i + 1][0]);
    }
    return dp[0][1];
}

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Instead of a full table, use three arrays (or variables) to represent index, index + 1, and index + 2.
  • Code (C++): 
    int stockCooldown(vector<int>& prices) {
    int n = prices.size();
    vector<int> front2(2, 0);
    vector<int> front1(2, 0);
    vector<int> cur(2, 0);

    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        cur[1] = max(-prices[i] + front1[0], 0 + front1[1]);
        cur[0] = max(prices[i] + front2[1], 0 + front1[0]);

        front2 = front1;
        front1 = cur;
    }
    return cur[1];
}

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][buy] is the maximum profit that can be obtained from day i onwards, given whether we are currently holding a stock (buy=0) or not (buy=1).

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N)\)
  • Space Complexity:
  • \(O(N)\) for Memoization/Tabulation.
  • \(O(1)\) for Space Optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Stocks: Variation of unlimited transactions with a state-skipping constraint.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Base Case: When using index + 2, ensure the base case handles index >= n, not just index == n.
  • State Transition: Remember that the cooldown only applies after a sell. You can buy any time you aren't holding a stock, unless you sold yesterday.

docs.title: Buy and Sell Stocks with Transaction Fee
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: You are given an array of stock prices and a transaction fee. You can buy and sell stocks unlimited times, but for every transaction (a buy and a sell together), a fixed fee is charged.
  • Goal: Maximize the total net profit.
  • Constraints: You cannot buy a stock if you already hold one. You must sell the current one before buying again.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This problem is an extension of "Buy and Sell Stocks II" (unlimited transactions).
  • The core logic remains: on any day, you can either buy/sell or skip.
  • To handle the fee, we subtract it exactly once per transaction. This can be done either when we perform a buy operation or when we perform a sell operation.

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Similar to Stock II, but subtract fee during either the buy or sell step.
  • Code (C++): 
    int solve(int index, int buy, vector<int>& prices, int fee, vector<vector<int>>& dp) {
    if (index == prices.size()) return 0;

    if (dp[index][buy] != -1) return dp[index][buy];

    if (buy) {
        // Buy (subtract price) or Skip
        return dp[index][buy] = max(-prices[index] + solve(index + 1, 0, prices, fee, dp),
                                   0 + solve(index + 1, 1, prices, fee, dp));
    } else {
        // Sell (add price AND subtract fee) or Skip
        return dp[index][buy] = max(prices[index] - fee + solve(index + 1, 1, prices, fee, dp),
                                   0 + solve(index + 1, 0, prices, fee, dp));
    }
}

docs.title: 2. Tabulation
docs.text:
• Explanation: Build the DP table bottom-up.
  • Code (C++): 
    int stockWithFee(vector<int>& prices, int fee) {
    int n = prices.size();
    vector<vector<int>> dp(n + 1, vector<int>(2, 0));

    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        // Buy case
        dp[i][1] = max(-prices[i] + dp[i + 1][0], 0 + dp[i + 1][1]);
        // Sell case
        dp[i][0] = max(prices[i] - fee + dp[i + 1][1], 0 + dp[i + 1][0]);
    }
    return dp[0][1];
}

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Only the next day's results are needed, so we can use two variables for buy and sell states.
  • Code (C++): 
    int stockWithFee(vector<int>& prices, int fee) {
    int n = prices.size();
    int nextBuy, nextSell, curBuy, curSell;
    nextBuy = nextSell = 0;

    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        curBuy = max(-prices[i] + nextSell, 0 + nextBuy);
        curSell = max(prices[i] - fee + nextBuy, 0 + nextSell);

        nextBuy = curBuy;
        nextSell = curSell;
    }
    return nextBuy;
}

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][buy] is the maximum profit that can be obtained from day i onwards, given whether we are currently holding a stock (buy=0) or not (buy=1).

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N)\)
  • Space Complexity:
  • \(O(N)\) for Memoization/Tabulation.
  • \(O(1)\) for Space Optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Stocks: Variation of unlimited transactions with a per-transaction cost.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Double Counting Fee: Ensure the fee is subtracted only once (either at buy or sell), not both.
  • Negative Profit: If the fee is higher than any possible profit, the result should be 0 (no transactions).

docs.title: Cherry Pickup II (3D DP on Grids)
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Alice and Bob start at the top row of an \(R \times C\) grid at \((0, 0)\) and \((0, C-1)\) respectively. They want to collect the maximum total chocolates while moving down to the last row.
  • Movement: In each step, both move to the next row (\(i+1\)) and can choose one of three columns: \(j-1, j, j+1\).
  • Rules:
  • If they land in the same cell, chocolates are collected only once.
  • If they land in different cells, both collection amounts are added.
  • Input: 2D grid of chocolates.
  • Output: Maximum chocolates collected.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• Simultaneous Movement: Alice and Bob move row-by-row together. This means their row index \(i\) is always the same.
  • State Representation: To capture the positions of both friends, we need the row \(i\) and their respective columns \(j_1\) and \(j_2\). This leads to a 3D state \((i, j_1, j_2)\).
  • Why Greedy Fails: Alice might pick a large number of chocolates now, but in doing so, she might force Bob into a path with very few chocolates, or vice versa. We must explore all 9 possible combinations of their moves \((3 \times 3)\).

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: Define f(i, j1, j2) as the max chocolates from row i to the bottom.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(i, j1, j2, r, c, grid):
    # Boundary Check
    if j1 < 0 or j1 >= c or j2 < 0 or j2 >= c:
        return -float('inf')

    # Base Case: Last Row
    if i == r - 1:
        if j1 == j2:
            return grid[i][j1]
        else:
            return grid[i][j1] + grid[i][j2]

    maxi = -float('inf')
    # Explore all 9 paths
    for dj1 in [-1, 0, 1]:
        for dj2 in [-1, 0, 1]:
            value = 0
            if j1 == j2:
                value = grid[i][j1]
            else:
                value = grid[i][j1] + grid[i][j2]

            value += solve(i + 1, j1 + dj1, j2 + dj2, r, c, grid)
            maxi = max(maxi, value)

    return maxi

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Store results in a 3D table dp[r][c][c].
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(i, j1, j2, r, c, grid, dp):
    if j1 < 0 or j1 >= c or j2 < 0 or j2 >= c:
        return -float('inf')
    if i == r - 1:
        return grid[i][j1] + (grid[i][j2] if j1 != j2 else 0)

    if dp[i][j1][j2] != -1:
        return dp[i][j1][j2]

    maxi = -float('inf')
    for dj1 in [-1, 0, 1]:
        for dj2 in [-1, 0, 1]:
            ans = grid[i][j1] + (grid[i][j2] if j1 != j2 else 0)
            ans += solve(i + 1, j1 + dj1, j2 + dj2, r, c, grid, dp)
            maxi = max(maxi, ans)

    dp[i][j1][j2] = maxi
    return dp[i][j1][j2]

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Fill the 3D table from the bottom row up to the top row.
  • Code (Python): 
    def cherryPickup(grid):
    r = len(grid)
    c = len(grid[0])
    dp = [[[0 for _ in range(c)] for _ in range(c)] for _ in range(r)]

    # Base case: last row
    for j1 in range(c):
        for j2 in range(c):
            if j1 == j2:
                dp[r-1][j1][j2] = grid[r-1][j1]
            else:
                dp[r-1][j1][j2] = grid[r-1][j1] + grid[r-1][j2]

    # Fill from r-2 to 0
    for i in range(r - 2, -1, -1):
        for j1 in range(c):
            for j2 in range(c):
                maxi = -float('inf')
                for dj1 in [-1, 0, 1]:
                    for dj2 in [-1, 0, 1]:
                        ans = grid[i][j1] + (grid[i][j2] if j1 != j2 else 0)
                        if 0 <= j1+dj1 < c and 0 <= j2+dj2 < c:
                            ans += dp[i+1][j1+dj1][j2+dj2]
                        else:
                            ans += -float('inf')
                        maxi = max(maxi, ans)
                dp[i][j1][j2] = maxi

    return dp[0][0][c-1]

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: The current row \(i\) only depends on row \(i+1\). We can reduce the 3D DP to two 2D layers: front[c][c] and cur[c][c].
  • Code (Python): 
    def cherryPickup(grid):
    r, c = len(grid), len(grid[0])
    front = [[0]*c for _ in range(c)]

    for j1 in range(c):
        for j2 in range(c):
            front[j1][j2] = grid[r-1][j1] + (grid[r-1][j2] if j1 != j2 else 0)

    for i in range(r-2, -1, -1):
        cur = [[0]*c for _ in range(c)]
        for j1 in range(c):
            for j2 in range(c):
                maxi = -float('inf')
                for dj1 in [-1, 0, 1]:
                    for dj2 in [-1, 0, 1]:
                        val = grid[i][j1] + (grid[i][j2] if j1 != j2 else 0)
                        if 0 <= j1+dj1 < c and 0 <= j2+dj2 < c:
                            val += front[j1+dj1][j2+dj2]
                        else:
                            val += -float('inf')
                        maxi = max(maxi, val)
                cur[j1][j2] = maxi
        front = cur

    return front[0][c-1]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j1][j2] represents the maximum chocolates Alice and Bob can collect starting from row i, with Alice at column j1 and Bob at column j2, until the last row.

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• \(f(i, j1, j2) = \text{chocolates at } (i, j1, j2) + \max(f(i+1, j1 \pm \{0, 1\}, j2 \pm \{0, 1\}))\)
  • Base Case: Row \(i = R-1\) returns the sum of chocolates at current columns.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(R \times C \times C \times 9) \approx O(R \times C^2)\).
  • Space Complexity:
  • \(O(R \times C^2)\) for 3D DP.
  • \(O(C^2)\) for Space Optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • 3D DP on Grids: Simultaneous movement of two entities.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Same Cell Counting: Forgetting to check if j1 == j2 and adding the chocolate count twice.
  • Out of Bounds: Failing to check boundaries for both Alice and Bob in all 9 movement combinations.
  • Return Type: Returning 0 for out-of-bounds instead of a very large negative number.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Cherry Pickup
  • Minimum Falling Path Sum
  • Minimum Path Sum

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: You are at the 0th stair and want to reach the \(N\)-th stair. Each time you can either climb 1 step or 2 steps.
  • Input: An integer \(N\) representing the number of stairs.
  • Output: The number of distinct ways to reach the \(N\)-th stair.
  • Constraints: \(N \ge 0\).

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• To reach the \(N\)-th stair, you must have come from either the \((N-1)\)-th stair (by taking a 1-step jump) or the \((N-2)\)-th stair (by taking a 2-step jump).
  • Total ways to reach \(N\) is the sum of ways to reach \((N-1)\) and ways to reach \((N-2)\).
  • This problem maps directly to the Fibonacci sequence.

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: Based on the recurrence \(f(n) = f(n-1) + f(n-2)\).
  • Code (Python): 
    def climb_stairs(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return 1
    return climb_stairs(n-1) + climb_stairs(n-2)

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use a DP array to store results of previously calculated stairs.
  • Code (Python): 
    def climb_stairs_memo(n, dp):
    if n <= 1:
        return 1
    if dp[n] != -1:
        return dp[n]
    dp[n] = climb_stairs_memo(n-1, dp) + climb_stairs_memo(n-2, dp)
    return dp[n]

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Fill an array from 0 to \(N\) iteratively.
  • Code (Python): 
    def climb_stairs_tab(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return 1
    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
    dp[0] = 1
    dp[1] = 1
    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]
    return dp[n]

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Only track the ways to reach the previous two stairs.
  • Code (Python): 
    def climb_stairs_opt(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return 1
    prev2, prev = 1, 1
    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        curr = prev + prev2
        prev2 = prev
        prev = curr
    return prev

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i] = Number of distinct ways to reach the \(i\)-th stair.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N)\)
  • Space: \(O(1)\) (with space optimization)

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text:
• 1D DP: Similar to Fibonacci.
  • Count Ways: The core goal is to find the total number of paths.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Base Case: Thinking \(f(0)=0\). There is 1 way to be at the 0th stair (by doing nothing).
  • Large N: For \(N > 45\), integers might overflow in some languages (though Python handles large integers automatically).

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Frog Jump
  • Min Cost Climbing Stairs

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given an array of coin denominations coins and a target amount, find the number of ways to make the change for the target. Each coin can be used an infinite number of times.
  • Input: Array coins, integer target.
  • Output: Total number of combinations that make up the target.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This is a variation of the "Count Subsets with Sum K" problem, but with infinite supply.
  • Pick/Not-Pick:
  • Not Pick: Move to the previous index: f(index - 1, target).
  • Pick: Since we have infinite supply, stay at the same index and reduce the target: f(index, target - coins[index]).
  • Base Case: At index 0, if the target is divisible by coins[0], return 1 (there is one way: pick coins[0] multiple times). Else, return 0.

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use a 2D dp table of size n x (target + 1).
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(index, target, coins, dp):
    if index == 0:
        return 1 if target % coins[0] == 0 else 0

    if dp[index][target] != -1:
        return dp[index][target]

    not_pick = solve(index - 1, target, coins, dp)
    pick = 0
    if coins[index] <= target:
        pick = solve(index, target - coins[index], coins, dp)

    dp[index][target] = pick + not_pick
    return dp[index][target]

docs.title: 2. Tabulation
docs.text:
• Code (Python): 
    def count_ways(n, target, coins):
    dp = [[0 for _ in range(target + 1)] for _ in range(n)]

    # Base case
    for t in range(target + 1):
        if t % coins[0] == 0:
            dp[0][t] = 1

    for i in range(1, n):
        for t in range(target + 1):
            not_pick = dp[i-1][t]
            pick = 0
            if coins[i] <= t:
                pick = dp[i][t - coins[i]]
            dp[i][t] = pick + not_pick

    return dp[n-1][target]

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization (Single Row)
docs.text:
• Explanation: To calculate dp[i][t], we need dp[i-1][t] (same column, previous row) and dp[i][t - coins[i]] (previous column, same row). Because we need the updated value from the same row, we should iterate forwards.
  • Code (Python): 
    def count_ways(n, target, coins):
    prev = [0] * (target + 1)
    for t in range(target + 1):
        if t % coins[0] == 0:
            prev[t] = 1

    for i in range(1, n):
        for t in range(target + 1):
            not_pick = prev[t]
            pick = 0
            if coins[i] <= t:
                pick = prev[t - coins[i]] # Using updated 'prev' for same row
            prev[t] = pick + not_pick

    return prev[target]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][t] is the number of ways to form sum t using coins from index 0 to i.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times Target)\)
  • Space Complexity: \(O(Target)\) with space optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • Infinite Supply Pattern: When an element can be reused, stay at the same index during the "Pick" step.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text: • Forwards vs Backwards Iteration: In 0/1 Knapsack (finite supply), we iterate backwards. In Unbounded Knapsack/Coin Change (infinite supply), we iterate forwards.

docs.title: Count Partitions With Given Difference (DP on Subsequences)
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given an array arr and an integer D, count the number of ways to partition the array into two subsets \(S_1\) and \(S_2\) such that \(sum(S_1) - sum(S_2) = D\).
  • Input: Array arr, difference D.
  • Output: Total number of such partitions.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• Mathematical Derivation:
  • \(s_1 + s_2 = \text{TotalSum}\)
  • \(s_1 - s_2 = D\)
  • Adding (1) and (2): \(2s_1 = \text{TotalSum} + D\)
  • \(\implies s_1 = \frac{\text{TotalSum} + D}{2}\)
  • The Transformation: The problem is reduced to finding the number of subsets whose sum is exactly \(\frac{\text{TotalSum} + D}{2}\).
  • Edge Cases:
  • \((\text{TotalSum} + D)\) must be even because \(s_1\) must be an integer.
  • \(\text{TotalSum} \ge D\) because subset sums cannot be negative.

docs.title: 1. Unified Logic (Using Count Subsets with Sum K)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Calculate the target \(s_1\) and call the "Count Subsets with Sum K" function.
  • Code (Python): 
    def countPartitionsWithDiff(arr, d):
    n = len(arr)
    total_sum = sum(arr)

    # Check if a valid partition sum s1 exists
    if (total_sum + d) % 2 != 0 or total_sum < d:
        return 0

    target = (total_sum + d) // 2
    return findWays(arr, target) # Reusing the Count Subsets function

def findWays(arr, k):
    n = len(arr)
    # Important: Handling zeros correctly is crucial here
    prev = [0] * (k + 1)

    if arr[0] == 0:
        prev[0] = 2 # Pick 0 or Not-pick 0
    else:
        prev[0] = 1 # Not-pick 0

    if arr[0] != 0 and arr[0] <= k:
        prev[arr[0]] = 1 # Pick arr[0]

    for i in range(1, n):
        cur = [0] * (k + 1)
        for target in range(0, k + 1):
            not_pick = prev[target]
            pick = 0
            if arr[i] <= target:
                pick = prev[target - arr[i]]
            cur[target] = (pick + not_pick) % (10**9 + 7)
        prev = cur

    return prev[k]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][target] represents the number of subsets of the first i elements that sum up to target.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times \text{Target})\) where \(\text{Target} = (\text{TotalSum} + D) / 2\).
  • Space Complexity: \(O(\text{Target})\) with space optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Subsequences: Converting a partition problem into a target sum problem.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• TotalSum < D: If the required difference is greater than the total sum of elements, no such partition exists.
  • Odd Sum: If TotalSum + D is odd, you cannot divide it by 2 to get an integer sum, so return 0.
  • Handling Zeros: Ensure your findWays function correctly counts subsets when elements are 0.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Count Subsets with Sum K
  • Target Sum (LeetCode) - This is exactly the same problem!

docs.title: Count Square Submatrices with All Ones
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
Given an n x m matrix filled with 0s and 1s, return the total number of square submatrices that have all ones. A square submatrix is defined as a submatrix where the number of rows equals the number of columns.

Input: A 2D grid matrix of size n x m. Output: An integer representing the total count of square submatrices.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• \(n, m \geq 1\)
  • Matrix contains only \(0\)s and \(1\)s.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
The core idea is to count how many squares end at each cell \((i, j)\), where \((i, j)\) is the bottom-right corner of the square.

  1. Why Brute Force Fails: A brute force approach would involve picking every possible top-left corner, every possible size, and checking if all elements in that square are 1s. This is computationally expensive (\(O(N^3)\) or \(O(N^4)\)).
  2. Thought Process:
    • If a cell \((i, j)\) is to be the bottom-right corner of a square of size \(k\), then the cells to its top \((i-1, j)\), left \((i, j-1)\), and top-left diagonal \((i-1, j-1)\) must all be bottom-right corners of squares of at least size \(k-1\).
    • The number of squares ending at \((i, j)\) is exactly equal to the side length of the largest square ending at \((i, j)\). For example, if the largest square ending at \((i, j)\) has a side of 3, then there are also squares of side 1 and 2 ending there.

docs.title: 1. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
We use a DP table where dp[i][j] stores the number of square submatrices ending at \((i, j)\).

  1. Initialize a dp table of the same size as the input matrix.
  2. Base Case: The first row and the first column of the dp table are the same as the input matrix because the largest square ending at those cells can only be of size 1 (if the cell is 1).
  3. Filling the Table: For each cell \((i, j)\) starting from \((1, 1)\):
    • If matrix[i][j] == 1: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i][j-1])
    • If matrix[i][j] == 0: dp[i][j] = 0
  4. Final Result: The total number of squares is the sum of all entries in the dp table.
def countSquares(matrix):
    n = len(matrix)
    m = len(matrix[0])

    # Initialize DP table
    dp = [[0] * m for _ in range(n)]

    # Fill base cases (first row and first column)
    for i in range(n):
        dp[i][0] = matrix[i][0]
    for j in range(m):
        dp[0][j] = matrix[0][j]

    # Fill the rest of the DP table
    for i in range(1, n):
        for j in range(1, m):
            if matrix[i][j] == 1:
                dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i][j-1])
            else:
                dp[i][j] = 0

    # Sum all values in dp table
    total_squares = sum(sum(row) for row in dp)
    return total_squares

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: dp[i][j] represents the side length of the largest square submatrix whose bottom-right corner is at cell (i, j). This value also corresponds to the total number of square submatrices ending at (i, j).

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
For matrix[i][j] == 1: $\(dp[i][j] = 1 + \min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i][j-1])\)$

For matrix[i][j] == 0: $\(dp[i][j] = 0\)$

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times M)\) where \(N\) and \(M\) are the dimensions of the matrix. We traverse the matrix once.
  • Space Complexity: \(O(N \times M)\) for the DP table. (Note: This can be optimized to \(O(M)\) by using only the previous row).

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: DP on Rectangles / Squares. This pattern involves using neighboring cells (top, left, diagonal) to determine the property of the current cell in a 2D grid.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Incorrect Base Case: Forgetting that the first row and column cannot form squares larger than size 1.
  • Off-by-one errors: Starting the loops from index 0 instead of 1 when using the recurrence.
  • Ignoring 0s: Not explicitly setting dp[i][j] = 0 when matrix[i][j] is 0 (though initialization handles this, it's a logical step).
  • Summing logic: Trying to find the max value in the DP table (which is for the "Maximal Square" problem) instead of summing all values.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
1. Maximal Square: Find the area of the largest square submatrix containing only 1s.
  2. Maximal Rectangle: Find the largest rectangle containing only 1s (uses a different histogram-based DP).
  3. Largest Plus Sign: Find the order of the largest axis-aligned plus sign of 1s.

docs.title: Count Subsets with Sum K (DP on Subsequences)
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given an array of non-negative integers and a target \(K\), count the number of subsets whose sum equals \(K\).
  • Input: An array arr and an integer K.
  • Output: Total number of subsets with sum \(K\).

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• The Core Idea: Similar to "Subset Sum Equals Target", but instead of checking for existence (OR), we sum the number of ways (Addition).
  • Pick/Not-Pick:
  • Not Pick: The number of ways remains the same as for the previous index with the same target.
  • Pick: If arr[index] <= target, the number of ways is what we had for the previous index with target - arr[index].
  • Total Ways: ways = count(index-1, target) + count(index-1, target - arr[index]).

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use a 2D dp table to store the number of ways for each (index, target) state.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(index, target, arr, dp):
    if target == 0:
        return 1
    if index == 0:
        return 1 if arr[0] == target else 0

    if dp[index][target] != -1:
        return dp[index][target]

    not_pick = solve(index - 1, target, arr, dp)
    pick = 0
    if arr[index] <= target:
        pick = solve(index - 1, target - arr[index], arr, dp)

    dp[index][target] = pick + not_pick
    return dp[index][target]

docs.title: 2. Tabulation
docs.text:
• Explanation: Build the dp table bottom-up.
  • Code (Python): 
    def findWays(arr, k):
    n = len(arr)
    dp = [[0 for _ in range(k + 1)] for _ in range(n)]

    for i in range(n):
        dp[i][0] = 1

    if arr[0] <= k:
        dp[0][arr[0]] = 1

    for i in range(1, n):
        for target in range(1, k + 1):
            not_pick = dp[i-1][target]
            pick = 0
            if arr[i] <= target:
                pick = dp[i-1][target - arr[i]]
            dp[i][target] = pick + not_pick

    return dp[n-1][k]

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use a single array prev to store results of the previous row.
  • Code (Python): 
    def findWays(arr, k):
    n = len(arr)
    prev = [0] * (k + 1)
    prev[0] = 1

    if arr[0] <= k:
        prev[arr[0]] = 1

    for i in range(1, n):
        cur = [0] * (k + 1)
        cur[0] = 1
        for target in range(1, k + 1):
            not_pick = prev[target]
            pick = 0
            if arr[i] <= target:
                pick = prev[target - arr[i]]
            cur[target] = pick + not_pick
        prev = cur

    return prev[k]

docs.title: 🧠 Handling Zeros
docs.text:
• The Problem: If the array contains zeros, the base case if target == 0 return 1 is incomplete because picking a zero doesn't change the sum but creates a new subset.
  • Modified Base Case (Recursion): 
    def solve(index, target, arr, dp):
    if index == 0:
        if target == 0 and arr[0] == 0:
            return 2  # Pick 0 or Not-pick 0
        if target == 0 or target == arr[0]:
            return 1
        return 0
    # ... rest of the code

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times K)\)
  • Space Complexity: \(O(N \times K)\) for Tabulation, \(O(K)\) for Space Optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Subsequences: Counting combinations using addition.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Initialising dp[i][0]: If elements can be zero, dp[i][0] is not simply 1. Use the modified base case logic.
  • Large Outputs: Often these problems require returning the result modulo \(10^9 + 7\).

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given two strings s1 and s2, count the number of distinct subsequences of s1 which equal s2.
  • Input: Strings s1 and s2.
  • Output: An integer representing the count of occurrences.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This is a string matching problem. We want to find how many ways we can "pick" characters from s1 to form s2.
  • At each step, if the characters match (s1[i] == s2[j]), we have two choices:
    1. Match them: Move both pointers (i-1, j-1).
    2. Don't match: Skip the character in s1 and look for a match later (i-1, j).
  • If they don't match, we only have one choice: skip the character in s1 (i-1, j).

docs.title: 1. Recursion + Memoization
docs.text:
def solve(i, j, s1, s2, dp):
    if j < 0: return 1 # s2 is exhausted, found a match
    if i < 0: return 0 # s1 is exhausted, no match possible

    if dp[i][j] != -1: return dp[i][j]

    if s1[i] == s2[j]:
        dp[i][j] = solve(i-1, j-1, s1, s2, dp) + solve(i-1, j, s1, s2, dp)
    else:
        dp[i][j] = solve(i-1, j, s1, s2, dp)
    return dp[i][j]

docs.title: 2. Tabulation
docs.text:
def numDistinct(s1, s2):
    n, m = len(s1), len(s2)
    dp = [[0] * (m + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]

    for i in range(n + 1): dp[i][0] = 1 # Empty s2 matches everything once

    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        for j in range(1, m + 1):
            if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]:
                dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + dp[i-1][j]
            else:
                dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j]
    return dp[n][m]

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization (1D Array)
docs.text:
Since dp[i][j] only depends on the previous row's values (dp[i-1]), we can optimize to \(O(M)\) space. 

def numDistinct(s1, s2):
    n, m = len(s1), len(s2)
    prev = [0] * (m + 1)
    prev[0] = 1

    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        for j in range(m, 0, -1): # Iterate backwards to use values from current 'prev'
            if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]:
                prev[j] = prev[j-1] + prev[j]
            # else: prev[j] stays prev[j]
    return prev[m]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j]: Number of distinct subsequences of s2[0...j-1] in s1[0...i-1].

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• If s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + dp[i-1][j]
  • Else: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j]

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N \times M)\)
  • Space: \(O(M)\) (Space Optimized)

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Strings (String Matching).

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Incorrect base cases: j < 0 should return 1, and i < 0 should return 0.
  • Forgetting the "don't match" case when s1[i] == s2[j]. You can still choose to use a different occurrence of that character later in s1.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Longest Common Subsequence
  • Edit Distance

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given two strings s1 and s2, find the minimum number of operations required to convert s1 to s2.
  • Operations allowed:
    1. Insert a character.
    2. Delete a character.
    3. Replace a character.
  • Input: Strings s1 and s2.
  • Output: Minimum edit distance (integer).

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This is the classic string matching problem. We compare characters from the end.
  • If characters match (s1[i] == s2[j]), 0 cost, move both pointers (i-1, j-1).
  • If they don't match, we try all three operations:
    1. Insert: Hypothetically insert s2[j] at the end of s1[i]. Now the last characters match, so we move only j-1. Cost: 1 + f(i, j-1).
    2. Delete: Remove s1[i]. Now we need to match s1[i-1] with s2[j]. Cost: 1 + f(i-1, j).
    3. Replace: Change s1[i] to s2[j]. Now they match, move both pointers. Cost: 1 + f(i-1, j-1).
  • We take the minimum of these three.

docs.title: 1. Recursion + Memoization
docs.text:
def solve(i, j, s1, s2, dp):
    if i < 0: return j + 1 # s1 empty, must insert all of s2
    if j < 0: return i + 1 # s2 empty, must delete all of s1

    if dp[i][j] != -1: return dp[i][j]

    if s1[i] == s2[j]:
        dp[i][j] = solve(i-1, j-1, s1, s2, dp)
    else:
        insert = 1 + solve(i, j-1, s1, s2, dp)
        delete = 1 + solve(i-1, j, s1, s2, dp)
        replace = 1 + solve(i-1, j-1, s1, s2, dp)
        dp[i][j] = min(insert, delete, replace)
    return dp[i][j]

docs.title: 2. Tabulation (Bottom-Up)
docs.text:
def minDistance(s1, s2):
    n, m = len(s1), len(s2)
    dp = [[0] * (m + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]

    for i in range(n + 1): dp[i][0] = i
    for j in range(m + 1): dp[0][j] = j

    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        for j in range(1, m + 1):
            if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]:
                dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]
            else:
                dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j], dp[i-1][j-1])
    return dp[n][m]

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization (1D Array)
docs.text:
def minDistance(s1, s2):
    n, m = len(s1), len(s2)
    prev = [j for j in range(m + 1)]
    curr = [0] * (m + 1)

    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        curr[0] = i
        for j in range(1, m + 1):
            if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]:
                curr[j] = prev[j-1]
            else:
                curr[j] = 1 + min(curr[j-1], prev[j], prev[j-1])
        prev = list(curr)
    return prev[m]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j]: Minimum operations to convert s1[0...i-1] to s2[0...j-1].

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• If s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]: f(i, j) = f(i-1, j-1)
  • Else: f(i, j) = 1 + min(f(i, j-1), f(i-1, j), f(i-1, j-1))

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N \times M)\)
  • Space: \(O(M)\) (Space Optimized)

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Strings (String Matching).

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Base cases: If one string is exhausted, the cost is the remaining length of the other string.
  • Mixing up the operations: (i, j-1) is Insert, (i-1, j) is Delete, (i-1, j-1) is Replace.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Longest Common Subsequence
  • Delete Operation for Two Strings
  • Wildcard Matching

docs.title: Evaluate Boolean Expression to True (Partition DP)
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given a boolean expression with operands T (True) and F (False) and operators & (AND), | (OR), and ^ (XOR). Count the number of ways to parenthesize the expression such that it evaluates to True.
  • Example: T ^ F | T
  • (T ^ F) | T -> T | T -> True
  • T ^ (F | T) -> T ^ T -> False
  • Total ways = 1.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• Partitioning: Like Matrix Chain Multiplication, we can partition the expression at every operator.
  • Multiple Results: To calculate the number of ways a sub-expression evaluates to True, we often need to know the number of ways it evaluates to False (e.g., for XOR: True ^ False = True, False ^ True = True).
  • DP State: We need a 3D DP state f(i, j, isTrue) representing the number of ways the sub-expression from index i to j evaluates to isTrue (1 for True, 0 for False).

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Iterate through all operators in the current range [i, j]. For each operator, calculate the number of ways to get True and False from the left and right sub-expressions.
  • Code (C++): 
    long long solve(int i, int j, int isTrue, string& s, vector<vector<vector<long long>>>& dp) {
    if (i > j) return 0;
    if (i == j) {
        if (isTrue) return s[i] == 'T';
        else return s[i] == 'F';
    }
    if (dp[i][j][isTrue] != -1) return dp[i][j][isTrue];

    long long ways = 0;
    for (int k = i + 1; k <= j - 1; k += 2) {
        long long lT = solve(i, k - 1, 1, s, dp);
        long long lF = solve(i, k - 1, 0, s, dp);
        long long rT = solve(k + 1, j, 1, s, dp);
        long long rF = solve(k + 1, j, 0, s, dp);

        if (s[k] == '&') {
            if (isTrue) ways = (ways + (lT * rT)) % 1000000007;
            else ways = (ways + (lT * rF) + (lF * rT) + (lF * rF)) % 1000000007;
        } else if (s[k] == '|') {
            if (isTrue) ways = (ways + (lT * rT) + (lT * rF) + (lF * rT)) % 1000000007;
            else ways = (ways + (lF * rF)) % 1000000007;
        } else if (s[k] == '^') {
            if (isTrue) ways = (ways + (lT * rF) + (lF * rT)) % 1000000007;
            else ways = (ways + (lT * rT) + (lF * rF)) % 1000000007;
        }
    }
    return dp[i][j][isTrue] = ways;
}

int countWays(int n, string s) {
    vector<vector<vector<long long>>> dp(n, vector<vector<long long>>(n, vector<long long>(2, -1)));
    return solve(0, n - 1, 1, s, dp);
}

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j][isTrue] is the number of ways the expression from index i to j evaluates to isTrue (where isTrue is 0 or 1).

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(2 \times N^3)\) where \(N\) is the length of the string. The factor of 2 comes from the isTrue state.
  • Space Complexity: \(O(2 \times N^2)\) for the DP table + \(O(N)\) for auxiliary stack space.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • Partition DP: Breaking the expression at each operator and combining results of sub-expressions.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Operator Selection: Operators are only at odd indices if indices start from 0 (T or F at 0, 2, 4...). Ensure the loop k iterates over operators only.
  • Missing Cases: Forgetting that "False" ways are needed to calculate "True" ways for some operators (like XOR and OR).
  • Modulo: Intermediate products can exceed int range, use long long and apply modulo at each addition.

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: A frog is at the 1st stair and wants to reach the \(N\)-th stair. Instead of just 1 or 2 steps, the frog can jump up to \(K\) steps (\(i+1, i+2, \dots, i+K\)). The cost of a jump is \(|H[i] - H[j]|\).
  • Input: Array of heights \(H\), number of stairs \(N\), and maximum jump distance \(K\).
  • Output: Minimum energy used to reach the last stair.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This is a generalization of the "Frog Jump" problem.
  • Instead of checking only two previous states (\(i-1, i-2\)), we now check \(K\) previous states.
  • The thought process remains the same: to reach state \(i\), we could have come from any state from \(i-1\) to \(i-K\).

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: Iterate through all possible jumps from \(1\) to \(K\) and find the minimum cost.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(i, k, heights):
    if i == 0:
        return 0

    min_steps = float('inf')
    for j in range(1, k + 1):
        if i - j >= 0:
            jump = solve(i - j, k, heights) + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-j])
            min_steps = min(min_steps, jump)
    return min_steps

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Cache the results of solve(i) in a DP array.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve_memo(i, k, heights, dp):
    if i == 0:
        return 0
    if dp[i] != -1:
        return dp[i]

    min_steps = float('inf')
    for j in range(1, k + 1):
        if i - j >= 0:
            jump = solve_memo(i - j, k, heights, dp) + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-j])
            min_steps = min(min_steps, jump)

    dp[i] = min_steps
    return dp[i]

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use a nested loop where the outer loop iterates through stairs \(1\) to \(N-1\) and the inner loop iterates through \(K\) possible jumps.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve_tab(n, k, heights):
    dp = [0] * n
    dp[0] = 0
    for i in range(1, n):
        min_steps = float('inf')
        for j in range(1, k + 1):
            if i - j >= 0:
                jump = dp[i-j] + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-j])
                min_steps = min(min_steps, jump)
        dp[i] = min_steps
    return dp[n-1]

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text: • Explanation: We can optimize space from \(O(N)\) to \(O(K)\) by keeping only the last \(K\) values. However, if \(K=N\) in the worst case, the space complexity remains \(O(N)\). For most interview purposes, the \(O(N)\) tabulation is acceptable.

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i] = Minimum energy required to reach the \(i\)-th stair.

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• \(f(i) = \min_{1 \le j \le K} \{ f(i-j) + |H[i] - H[i-j]| \}\)
  • Base Case: \(f(0) = 0\)

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N \times K)\)
  • Space: \(O(N)\) (Tabulation/Memoization)

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • 1D DP: Min/Max Path Cost with \(K\) transitions.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Not checking i - j >= 0 before accessing the array or calling recursion.
  • Using a fixed number of variables (like prev1, prev2) instead of a loop when \(K\) is dynamic.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Frog Jump
  • Climbing Stairs

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: A frog is at the 1st stair and wants to reach the \(N\)-th stair. Each stair has a height \(H[i]\). The frog can jump from stair \(i\) to \(i+1\) or \(i+2\). The energy lost in a jump from \(i\) to \(j\) is \(|H[i] - H[j]|\).
  • Input: Array of heights and \(N\).
  • Output: Minimum total energy to reach the \(N\)-th stair.
  • Constraints: \(N\) up to \(10^5\).

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• The frog wants the path with the minimum energy cost.
  • A greedy approach (choosing the smallest jump cost at each step) fails because a slightly more expensive jump now might lead to a much cheaper path overall in the future.
  • We must explore all possibilities (recursion) and optimize using DP.

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: \(f(i)\) is the min energy to reach stair \(i\) from stair 0.
  • Code (Python): 
    def frog_jump(i, heights):
    if i == 0:
        return 0
    jump_one = frog_jump(i-1, heights) + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-1])
    jump_two = float('inf')
    if i > 1:
        jump_two = frog_jump(i-2, heights) + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-2])
    return min(jump_one, jump_two)

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Store the minimum energy to reach each stair in a dp array.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(i, heights, dp):
    if i == 0:
        return 0
    if dp[i] != -1:
        return dp[i]

    jump_one = solve(i-1, heights, dp) + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-1])
    jump_two = float('inf')
    if i > 1:
        jump_two = solve(i-2, heights, dp) + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-2])

    dp[i] = min(jump_one, jump_two)
    return dp[i]

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Iteratively compute the minimum energy for each stair from 0 to \(N-1\).
  • Code (Python): 
    def frog_jump_tab(n, heights):
    dp = [0] * n
    dp[0] = 0
    for i in range(1, n):
        fs = dp[i-1] + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-1])
        ss = float('inf')
        if i > 1:
            ss = dp[i-2] + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-2])
        dp[i] = min(fs, ss)
    return dp[n-1]

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: To calculate dp[i], we only need dp[i-1] and dp[i-2].
  • Code (Python): 
    def frog_jump_opt(n, heights):
    prev, prev2 = 0, 0
    for i in range(1, n):
        fs = prev + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-1])
        ss = float('inf')
        if i > 1:
            ss = prev2 + abs(heights[i] - heights[i-2])

        curr = min(fs, ss)
        prev2 = prev
        prev = curr
    return prev

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i] = Minimum energy required to reach the \(i\)-th stair from the 0th stair.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N)\)
  • Space: \(O(1)\) (Space Optimized)

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • 1D DP: Min/Max Path Cost.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Not handling the i > 1 condition for the second jump, which leads to index out of bounds.
  • Incorrectly initializing jump_two with 0 instead of infinity when it's not possible.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Frog Jump with K Distance
  • Climbing Stairs

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: You are given an \(M \times N\) grid. You start at the top-left corner \((0, 0)\) and want to reach the bottom-right corner \((M-1, N-1)\). You can only move either Down or Right at any point in time.
  • Input: Two integers \(M\) and \(N\).
  • Output: The total number of unique paths to reach the destination.
  • Constraints: \(M, N \ge 1\).

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• At any cell \((i, j)\), there are two ways to reach it:
    1. From the cell above: \((i-1, j)\)
    2. From the cell to the left: \((i, j-1)\)
  • The total number of ways to reach \((i, j)\) is the sum of ways to reach \((i-1, j)\) and \((i, j-1)\).
  • Base Case: There is only 1 way to reach the starting cell \((0, 0)\).

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: Start from \((M-1, N-1)\) and recursively move up and left until \((0, 0)\) is reached.
  • Code (Python): 
    def count_paths(i, j):
    if i == 0 and j == 0:
        return 1
    if i < 0 or j < 0:
        return 0

    up = count_paths(i - 1, j)
    left = count_paths(i, j - 1)

    return up + left

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Store the results of subproblems in a 2D array dp[M][N] to avoid redundant calculations.
  • Code (Python): 
    def count_paths_memo(m, n):
    dp = [[-1] * n for _ in range(m)]

    def solve(i, j):
        if i == 0 and j == 0:
            return 1
        if i < 0 or j < 0:
            return 0
        if dp[i][j] != -1:
            return dp[i][j]

        dp[i][j] = solve(i - 1, j) + solve(i, j - 1)
        return dp[i][j]

    return solve(m - 1, n - 1)

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Fill the DP table iteratively starting from dp[0][0].
  • Code (Python): 
    def count_paths_tab(m, n):
    dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(m)]

    for i in range(m):
        for j in range(n):
            if i == 0 and j == 0:
                dp[i][j] = 1
            else:
                up = dp[i-1][j] if i > 0 else 0
                left = dp[i][j-1] if j > 0 else 0
                dp[i][j] = up + left

    return dp[m-1][n-1]

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: To calculate the current row's values, we only need the values from the previous row and the value from the left cell in the current row.
  • Code (Python): 
    def count_paths_opt(m, n):
    prev = [0] * n
    for i in range(m):
        curr = [0] * n
        for j in range(n):
            if i == 0 and j == 0:
                curr[j] = 1
            else:
                up = prev[j]
                left = curr[j-1] if j > 0 else 0
                curr[j] = up + left
        prev = curr
    return prev[n-1]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j] = Total number of unique paths from \((0, 0)\) to \((i, j)\).

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(M \times N)\)
  • Space: \(O(N)\) (Space Optimized) or \(O(M \times N)\) (Tabulation/Memoization)

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • 2D DP: DP on Grids (Path Counting).

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Not handling out-of-bounds cases (i < 0 or j < 0) in recursion.
  • Forgetting the base case dp[0][0] = 1.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Unique Paths II (with obstacles)
  • Minimum Path Sum
  • Cherry Pickup

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: A professional robber plans to rob houses along a circular street. Each house has a certain amount of money. You cannot rob two adjacent houses as they are connected to a security system. Since the street is circular, the first and last houses are also adjacent.
  • Input: An array of non-negative integers money.
  • Output: The maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police.
  • Constraints:
    • The houses are arranged in a circle.
    • Adjacent houses (including first and last) cannot be robbed together.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This problem is a direct variation of House Robber I (Maximum Sum of Non-Adjacent Elements).
  • In House Robber I, the houses were in a straight line. Here, the circular constraint means if we pick the first house, we cannot pick the last house, and vice versa.
  • The Core Idea:
    1. The answer will either NOT include the first house.
    2. Or the answer will NOT include the last house.
  • We can simply run the House Robber I algorithm twice:
    • Once on the array excluding the first element (arr[1:]).
    • Once on the array excluding the last element (arr[0:-1]).
  • The final result is the maximum of these two results.

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use the standard non-adjacent sum logic but on two different slices of the array.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(arr):
    def helper(i, sub_arr):
        if i == 0: return sub_arr[0]
        if i < 0: return 0

        pick = sub_arr[i] + helper(i - 2, sub_arr)
        not_pick = 0 + helper(i - 1, sub_arr)
        return max(pick, not_pick)

    n = len(arr)
    if n == 1: return arr[0]

    # Case 1: Exclude first, Case 2: Exclude last
    res1 = helper(n - 2, arr[1:])
    res2 = helper(n - 2, arr[:-1])
    return max(res1, res2)

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Cache results for the sub-problems to optimize recursion.
  • Code (Python): 
    def rob(nums):
    def solve_memo(i, arr, dp):
        if i == 0: return arr[0]
        if i < 0: return 0
        if dp[i] != -1: return dp[i]

        pick = arr[i] + solve_memo(i - 2, arr, dp)
        not_pick = 0 + solve_memo(i - 1, arr, dp)

        dp[i] = max(pick, not_pick)
        return dp[i]

    n = len(nums)
    if n == 1: return nums[0]

    arr1 = nums[1:]
    arr2 = nums[:-1]

    dp1 = [-1] * (n - 1)
    dp2 = [-1] * (n - 1)

    return max(solve_memo(n-2, arr1, dp1), solve_memo(n-2, arr2, dp2))

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Build the DP table for both cases.
  • Code (Python): 
    def rob(nums):
    def solve_tab(arr):
        n = len(arr)
        dp = [0] * n
        dp[0] = arr[0]
        for i in range(1, n):
            pick = arr[i]
            if i > 1: pick += dp[i-2]
            not_pick = dp[i-1]
            dp[i] = max(pick, not_pick)
        return dp[n-1]

    n = len(nums)
    if n == 1: return nums[0]
    return max(solve_tab(nums[1:]), solve_tab(nums[:-1]))

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Since House Robber I only needs the last two results, we can reduce space to \(O(1)\).
  • Code (Python): 
    def rob(nums):
    def solve_opt(arr):
        prev = arr[0]
        prev2 = 0
        for i in range(1, len(arr)):
            pick = arr[i] + prev2
            not_pick = prev
            curr = max(pick, not_pick)
            prev2 = prev
            prev = curr
        return prev

    n = len(nums)
    if n == 1: return nums[0]
    if n == 0: return 0

    # Case 1: Exclude first, Case 2: Exclude last
    res1 = solve_opt(nums[1:])
    res2 = solve_opt(nums[:-1])

    return max(res1, res2)

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i] = Maximum money robbed considering houses up to index \(i\) in a linear arrangement.

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• \(f(i) = \max(arr[i] + f(i-2), f(i-1))\)
  • For House Robber II: \(\text{Result} = \max(f(arr[1 \dots N-1]), f(arr[0 \dots N-2]))\)

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N)\) (We traverse the array twice).
  • Space: \(O(1)\) (Space optimized version).

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • 1D DP: DP on Subsequences (Circular version).

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Not handling the edge case where the array has only 1 element.
  • Including both the first and last house in the same sub-problem logic.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• House Robber I
  • Maximum Sum of Non-Adjacent Elements

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Calculate the \(N\)-th Fibonacci number.
  • Input: An integer \(N\).
  • Output: The \(N\)-th Fibonacci number where \(F(0)=0, F(1)=1\) and \(F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)\).
  • Constraints: \(N \ge 0\). Standard recursion fails for large \(N\) due to exponential time complexity.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• The Core Idea: "Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it."
  • Evolution: Simple recursion recalculates the same subproblems multiple times (e.g., \(F(2)\) is calculated many times when finding \(F(5)\)). This is known as Overlapping Subproblems.
  • Solution: Store the results of subproblems so they are calculated only once.

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: A direct implementation of the recurrence relation. It has a high cost because it branches out into a tree of redundant calls.
  • Code (Python): 
    def fibonacci(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return n
    return fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Store the result of each function call in an array or hash map. Before computing \(F(n)\), check if it's already in the storage.
  • Code (Python): 
    def fibonacci_memo(n, dp):
    if n <= 1:
        return n
    if dp[n] != -1:
        return dp[n]
    dp[n] = fibonacci_memo(n-1, dp) + fibonacci_memo(n-2, dp)
    return dp[n]

n = 5
dp = [-1] * (n + 1)
print(fibonacci_memo(n, dp))

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use a table (array) to store results starting from the base cases (\(0\) and \(1\)) and iteratively fill the table up to \(N\).
  • Code (Python): 
    def fibonacci_tab(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return n
    dp = [-1] * (n + 1)
    dp[0], dp[1] = 0, 1
    for i in range(2, n + 1):
