        if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]:
            lcs_str[index] = s1[i-1]
            index -= 1
            i -= 1
            j -= 1
        elif dp[i-1][j] > dp[i][j-1]:
            i -= 1
        else:
            j -= 1

    return "".join(lcs_str)

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j] is the length of the LCS of S1[0...i-1] and S2[0...j-1].

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times M)\) to fill the table + \(O(N + M)\) to backtrack.
  • Space Complexity: \(O(N \times M)\) to store the DP table (required for backtracking).

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP Backtracking: Using a filled DP table to reconstruct the optimal solution.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• String Construction: Since we backtrack from the end, either append characters and reverse the string at the end, or fill a fixed-size array from right to left.
  • Equality in Max: If dp[i-1][j] == dp[i][j-1], moving in either direction is fine, but it might lead to different (equally valid) LCS strings.

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Beyond finding the length of the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS), the goal is to actually reconstruct and print the elements of one such subsequence.
  • Goal: Return the actual elements of the LIS in the correct order.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• To print the LIS, we need a way to backtrack.
  • The standard \(O(N^2)\) tabulation approach (where dp[i] is the LIS ending at index i) is better suited for this than the "pick/not-pick" 2D DP table.
  • We maintain a hash array to store the index of the previous element that contributed to the current LIS length.

docs.title: 1. Tabulation Algorithm (\(O(N^2)\))
docs.text:
• Explanation:
  • Initialize a dp array of size \(N\) with 1s (each element is an LIS of length 1).
  • For each index i, iterate through all previous indices j (from 0 to i-1).
  • If arr[j] < arr[i] and 1 + dp[j] > dp[i], update dp[i] = 1 + dp[j].
  • Code (C++): 
    int longestIncreasingSubsequence(int arr[], int n) {
    vector<int> dp(n, 1);
    int maxi = 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        for (int prev = 0; prev < i; prev++) {
            if (arr[prev] < arr[i]) {
                dp[i] = max(dp[i], 1 + dp[prev]);
            }
        }
        maxi = max(maxi, dp[i]);
    }
    return maxi;
}

docs.title: 2. Printing the LIS (Backtracking)
docs.text:
• Explanation:
  • Use a hash array to store the index of the predecessor of each element in its LIS.
  • Keep track of the lastIndex where the maximum LIS length was found.
  • Backtrack from lastIndex using the hash array until you reach an element that is its own predecessor.
  • Code (C++): 
    void printLIS(int arr[], int n) {
    vector<int> dp(n, 1);
    vector<int> hash(n);
    int maxi = 1;
    int lastIndex = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        hash[i] = i; // Initializing hash with its own index
        for (int prev = 0; prev < i; prev++) {
            if (arr[prev] < arr[i] && 1 + dp[prev] > dp[i]) {
                dp[i] = 1 + dp[prev];
                hash[i] = prev;
            }
        }
        if (dp[i] > maxi) {
            maxi = dp[i];
            lastIndex = i;
        }
    }

    vector<int> lis;
    lis.push_back(arr[lastIndex]);
    while (hash[lastIndex] != lastIndex) {
        lastIndex = hash[lastIndex];
        lis.push_back(arr[lastIndex]);
    }
    reverse(lis.begin(), lis.end());

    for (auto it : lis) cout << it << " ";
}

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text:
• dp[i] is the length of the longest increasing subsequence that ends specifically at index i.
  • hash[i] stores the index of the previous element in the LIS that ends at index i.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N^2)\) to fill the dp and hash arrays, plus \(O(N)\) for backtracking. Total \(O(N^2)\).
  • Space Complexity: \(O(N)\) to store dp, hash, and the resulting lis vector.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • LIS Reconstruction: Using an auxiliary array (hash) to store state transitions for backtracking.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Initial Values: Forgetting that dp[i] should start at 1 and hash[i] should start at i.
  • Max Length Index: Forgetting that the LIS might not end at the very last element of the input array. You must find the global maximum in the dp array.
  • Strictly Increasing: The condition arr[prev] < arr[i] ensures strictly increasing.

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given a rod of length \(N\) and an array price where price[i] is the price of a rod of length \(i+1\). Cut the rod into smaller pieces to maximize the total value.
  • Input: Integer N, Array price.
  • Output: Maximum total value.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This is a classic Unbounded Knapsack problem.
  • Mapping to Knapsack:
  • Bag Capacity = Rod length N.
  • Item Weight = Length of the piece (\(1, 2, \dots, N\)).
  • Item Value = price[length - 1].
  • Each length can be used multiple times (Infinite Supply).
  • Pick/Not-Pick:
  • Not Pick: Try making pieces using lengths only up to index - 1.
  • Pick: Take a piece of current length and stay at the index (since you can take another piece of the same length).

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization
docs.text:
• Code (Python): 
    def solve(index, current_n, price, dp):
    if index == 0:
        # At index 0, piece length is 1. We take current_n pieces.
        return current_n * price[0]

    if dp[index][current_n] != -1:
        return dp[index][current_n]

    not_pick = solve(index - 1, current_n, price, dp)
    pick = -1e9
    rod_length = index + 1
    if rod_length <= current_n:
        pick = price[index] + solve(index, current_n - rod_length, price, dp)

    dp[index][current_n] = max(pick, not_pick)
    return dp[index][current_n]

docs.title: 2. Tabulation
docs.text:
• Code (Python): 
    def cut_rod(n, price):
    dp = [[0 for _ in range(n + 1)] for _ in range(n)]

    for length in range(n + 1):
        dp[0][length] = length * price[0]

    for i in range(1, n):
        for length in range(n + 1):
            not_pick = dp[i-1][length]
            pick = -1e9
            rod_len = i + 1
            if rod_len <= length:
                pick = price[i] + dp[i][length - rod_len]
            dp[i][length] = max(pick, not_pick)

    return dp[n-1][n]

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization (Single Row)
docs.text:
• Code (Python): 
    def cut_rod(n, price):
    prev = [0] * (n + 1)

    for length in range(n + 1):
        prev[length] = length * price[0]

    for i in range(1, n):
        # Forward iteration for infinite supply
        for length in range(n + 1):
            not_pick = prev[length]
            pick = -1e9
            rod_len = i + 1
            if rod_len <= length:
                pick = price[i] + prev[length - rod_len]
            prev[length] = max(pick, not_pick)

    return prev[n]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][L] is the maximum price obtainable using pieces of lengths up to i+1 for a rod of length L.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times N)\)
  • Space Complexity: \(O(N)\) with single row optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • Unbounded Knapsack Pattern: Items can be reused; capacity is the total rod length.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Indexing: Remember that the length of the piece at index is index + 1.
  • Base Case: At index 0, the piece length is 1, so the maximum value is total_length * price_of_length_1.

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given two strings s1 and s2, find the shortest string that has both s1 and s2 as subsequences.
  • Input: Two strings s1 and s2.
  • Output: The shortest common supersequence string.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• A "supersequence" contains all characters of both strings in their original relative order.
  • The simplest supersequence is just s1 + s2, but it's not the shortest.
  • To make it shortest, we should include the characters that are common to both strings (the Longest Common Subsequence) only once.
  • Formula for Length: len(s1) + len(s2) - len(LCS(s1, s2)).
  • To reconstruct the string, we use the DP table from LCS and backtrack.

docs.title: 1. Tabulation (LCS Based)
docs.text:
We first fill the standard LCS table. 

def get_lcs_table(s1, s2):
    n, m = len(s1), len(s2)
    dp = [[0] * (m + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        for j in range(1, m + 1):
            if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]:
                dp[i][j] = 1 + dp[i-1][j-1]
            else:
                dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
    return dp

docs.title: 2. Reconstructing the String
docs.text:
Backtrack from dp[n][m] to dp[0][0]. 

def shortestCommonSupersequence(s1, s2):
    n, m = len(s1), len(s2)
    dp = get_lcs_table(s1, s2)

    i, j = n, m
    ans = ""
    while i > 0 and j > 0:
        if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]:
            ans += s1[i-1]
            i -= 1
            j -= 1
        elif dp[i-1][j] > dp[i][j-1]:
            ans += s1[i-1]
            i -= 1
        else:
            ans += s2[j-1]
            j -= 1

    # Add remaining characters
    while i > 0:
        ans += s1[i-1]
        i -= 1
    while j > 0:
        ans += s2[j-1]
        j -= 1

    return ans[::-1]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j]: Length of the Longest Common Subsequence between s1[0...i-1] and s2[0...j-1].

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• If s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = 1 + dp[i-1][j-1]
  • Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N \times M)\) to build the table and \(O(N+M)\) to backtrack.
  • Space: \(O(N \times M)\) for the DP table.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Strings (LCS Variation).

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Forgetting to add the remaining characters after the while i > 0 and j > 0 loop finishes.
  • Incorrectly choosing which character to add when s1[i-1] != s2[j-1]. We should move in the direction of the maximum LCS value.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Longest Common Subsequence
  • Printing Longest Common Subsequence
  • Shortest Common Supersequence Length

docs.title: Subset Sum Equals Target (DP on Subsequences)
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given an array of \(N\) positive integers and a target \(K\), check if there exists a subset whose sum equals \(K\).
  • Subset vs Subsequence: A subset is a selection of elements from the array (order doesn't matter for sum), whereas a subsequence follows the array's order. For this problem, both terms are used interchangeably.
  • Input: An array arr and an integer K.
  • Output: Boolean (True if a subset exists, False otherwise).

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• The Core Idea: For every element in the array, we have a simple binary choice:
  • Pick (Take): Include the element in our subset and reduce the target.
  • Not Pick (Not Take): Skip the element and keep the target the same.
  • Why Greedy Fails: There is no uniform way to pick elements. Picking the largest available element might prevent you from forming the target with smaller elements, and vice versa.
  • Thought Process:
  • We represent the state as f(index, target), meaning: "Can we form target using elements from 0 to index?"
  • If we "take" arr[index], the new state is f(index - 1, target - arr[index]).
  • If we "not take", the new state is f(index - 1, target).

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: Try both "take" and "not take" for every index. If either returns True, then the result for that state is True.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(index, target, arr):
    if target == 0:
        return True
    if index == 0:
        return arr[0] == target

    not_take = solve(index - 1, target, arr)
    take = False
    if arr[index] <= target:
        take = solve(index - 1, target - arr[index], arr)

    return take or not_take

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Use a 2D dp table initialized with -1 to store the results of (index, target).
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(index, target, arr, dp):
    if target == 0:
        return True
    if index == 0:
        return arr[0] == target

    if dp[index][target] != -1:
        return dp[index][target]

    not_take = solve(index - 1, target, arr, dp)
    take = False
    if arr[index] <= target:
        take = solve(index - 1, target - arr[index], arr, dp)

    dp[index][target] = take or not_take
    return dp[index][target]

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Create a boolean table and fill it based on the base cases.
  • Code (Python): 
    def subsetSumToK(n, k, arr):
    dp = [[False for _ in range(k + 1)] for _ in range(n)]

    # Base case 1: Target is 0
    for i in range(n):
        dp[i][0] = True

    # Base case 2: Index 0
    if arr[0] <= k:
        dp[0][arr[0]] = True

    for i in range(1, n):
        for target in range(1, k + 1):
            not_take = dp[i-1][target]
            take = False
            if arr[i] <= target:
                take = dp[i-1][target - arr[i]]
            dp[i][target] = take or not_take

    return dp[n-1][k]

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Each row in the dp table only depends on the row above it. We can use a single array prev to keep track of the results for index - 1.
  • Code (Python): 
    def subsetSumToK(n, k, arr):
    prev = [False] * (k + 1)
    prev[0] = True

    if arr[0] <= k:
        prev[arr[0]] = True

    for i in range(1, n):
        cur = [False] * (k + 1)
        cur[0] = True # Target 0 is always possible
        for target in range(1, k + 1):
            not_take = prev[target]
            take = False
            if arr[i] <= target:
                take = prev[target - arr[i]]
            cur[target] = take or not_take
        prev = cur

    return prev[k]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][target] is a boolean representing whether a subset with sum target can be formed using elements from the first i indices (0 to i) of the array.

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• f(i, target) = f(i-1, target) || f(i-1, target - arr[i]) (if \(arr[i] \le target\))
  • Base Cases:
  • if target == 0: True
  • if i == 0: True if arr[0] == target, else False

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times K)\) where \(N\) is the number of elements and \(K\) is the target sum.
  • Space Complexity:
  • \(O(N \times K)\) for Tabulation/Memoization.
  • \(O(K)\) for Space Optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Subsequences: The Pick/Not-Pick pattern.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Target 0 in Tabulation: Always ensure dp[i][0] = True because an empty subset can always form a sum of 0.
  • Boundary Check: Forgetting to check arr[i] <= target before accessing dp[i-1][target - arr[i]].
  • Boolean vs Int: In some languages, dp should be an integer table for memoization to handle the "unvisited" state (-1).

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Partition Equal Subset Sum
  • Target Sum
  • Coin Change 2

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given an array of integers arr and a target, assign a + or - sign to each integer and sum them up. Find the number of ways to assign signs such that the sum equals the target.
  • Input: Array arr, integer target.
  • Output: Total number of ways to achieve the target.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• Assigning + and - signs is equivalent to partitioning the array into two subsets, \(S_1\) and \(S_2\), where:
  • \(S_1\) is the set of elements with a + sign.
  • \(S_2\) is the set of elements with a - sign.
  • The equation becomes: \(Sum(S_1) - Sum(S_2) = \text{target}\).
  • We also know: \(Sum(S_1) + Sum(S_2) = \text{TotalSum}\).
  • Adding these two equations: \(2 \times Sum(S_1) = \text{TotalSum} + \text{target}\).
  • Therefore, \(Sum(S_1) = \frac{\text{TotalSum} + \text{target}}{2}\).
  • The problem reduces to: Count the number of subsets with sum \(K\), where \(K = \frac{\text{TotalSum} + \text{target}}{2}\).

docs.title: 1. Reducing the Problem
docs.text:
• Check if \((\text{TotalSum} + \text{target})\) is even and if \(\text{TotalSum} \ge \text{target}\). If not, return 0.
  • Let new_target = (TotalSum + target) // 2.
  • Solve "Count Subsets with Sum new_target".

docs.title: 2. Tabulation (Count Subsets with Sum K)
docs.text:
• Code (Python): 
    def count_subsets_with_sum_k(arr, k):
    n = len(arr)
    dp = [[0 for _ in range(k + 1)] for _ in range(n)]

    # Base Case: Sum 0
    if arr[0] == 0: dp[0][0] = 2 # Pick 0 or Not Pick 0
    else: dp[0][0] = 1 # Not Pick

    # Base Case: First element
    if arr[0] != 0 and arr[0] <= k:
        dp[0][arr[0]] = 1

    for i in range(1, n):
        for target in range(k + 1):
            not_pick = dp[i-1][target]
            pick = 0
            if arr[i] <= target:
                pick = dp[i-1][target - arr[i]]
            dp[i][target] = pick + not_pick

    return dp[n-1][k]

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Code (Python): 
    def target_sum(arr, target):
    total_sum = sum(arr)
    if (total_sum + target) % 2 != 0 or total_sum < abs(target):
        return 0

    k = (total_sum + target) // 2
    n = len(arr)
    prev = [0] * (k + 1)

    if arr[0] == 0: prev[0] = 2
    else: prev[0] = 1

    if arr[0] != 0 and arr[0] <= k:
        prev[arr[0]] = 1

    for i in range(1, n):
        cur = [0] * (k + 1)
        for t in range(k + 1):
            not_pick = prev[t]
            pick = 0
            if arr[i] <= t:
                pick = prev[t - arr[i]]
            cur[t] = pick + not_pick
        prev = cur
    return prev[k]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j] is the number of ways to form sum j using elements from index 0 to i.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times K)\), where \(K = \frac{\text{TotalSum} + \text{target}}{2}\).
  • Space Complexity: \(O(K)\) with space optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Subsequences: Specifically, "Count Subsets with Given Sum" variation.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Zeroes in Array: If the array contains zeroes, the base case dp[i][0] is not simply 1. A zero can be either picked or not picked, doubling the ways.
  • Edge Cases: \((\text{TotalSum} + \text{target})\) must be non-negative and even.

docs.title: Triangle (Fixed Starting Point and Variable Ending Point)
🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given a triangular array, return the minimum path sum from the top to the bottom row.
  • Movement: From index j in row i, you can move to index j or j + 1 in row i + 1.
  • Input: A 2D list triangle where the \(i\)-th row has \(i+1\) elements.
  • Output: The minimum path sum (integer).
  • Constraints: Fixed starting point at (0, 0), variable ending point anywhere in the last row.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• Why Greedy Fails: Choosing the immediate minimum at each step might lead to a path with very large values later on. We need to consider all possible paths.
  • Thought Process:
  • We start at a single point (0, 0) and want to reach any point in the last row.
  • Since the ending point is variable, starting the recursion from the top (0, 0) down to the bottom is more intuitive than starting from the bottom up.
  • At any cell (i, j), we have two choices: go down to (i+1, j) or go diagonally to (i+1, j+1).

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: Start from (0, 0) and recursively calculate the min path sum for the two possible movements.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(i, j, n, triangle):
    if i == n - 1:
        return triangle[i][j]

    down = triangle[i][j] + solve(i + 1, j, n, triangle)
    diagonal = triangle[i][j] + solve(i + 1, j + 1, n, triangle)

    return min(down, diagonal)

def minimumPathSum(triangle, n):
    return solve(0, 0, n, triangle)

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Store the results of subproblems in a dp table to avoid redundant calculations.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve(i, j, n, triangle, dp):
    if i == n - 1:
        return triangle[i][j]

    if dp[i][j] != -1:
        return dp[i][j]

    down = triangle[i][j] + solve(i + 1, j, n, triangle, dp)
    diagonal = triangle[i][j] + solve(i + 1, j + 1, n, triangle, dp)

    dp[i][j] = min(down, diagonal)
    return dp[i][j]

def minimumPathSum(triangle, n):
    dp = [[-1 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]
    return solve(0, 0, n, triangle, dp)

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Start from the bottom row and fill the dp table upwards to the top.
  • Code (Python): 
    def minimumPathSum(triangle, n):
    dp = [[0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]

    # Base case: last row
    for j in range(n):
        dp[n-1][j] = triangle[n-1][j]

    # Fill upwards
    for i in range(n - 2, -1, -1):
        for j in range(i + 1):
            down = triangle[i][j] + dp[i+1][j]
            diagonal = triangle[i][j] + dp[i+1][j+1]
            dp[i][j] = min(down, diagonal)

    return dp[0][0]

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Each row only depends on the row directly below it. We can use a single array to store the "next" row's results.
  • Code (Python): 
    def minimumPathSum(triangle, n):
    # Start with the last row
    front = [triangle[n-1][j] for j in range(n)]

    for i in range(n - 2, -1, -1):
        cur = [0 for _ in range(i + 1)]
        for j in range(i + 1):
            down = triangle[i][j] + front[j]
            diagonal = triangle[i][j] + front[j+1]
            cur[j] = min(down, diagonal)
        front = cur

    return front[0]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j] represents the minimum path sum to reach the bottom row starting from cell (i, j).

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• f(i, j) = triangle[i][j] + min(f(i+1, j), f(i+1, j+1))
  • Base Case: f(n-1, j) = triangle[n-1][j] for all \(j \in [0, n-1]\).

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times N)\) where \(N\) is the number of rows. We visit each cell once.
  • Space Complexity:
  • \(O(N \times N)\) for Memoization/Tabulation.
  • \(O(N)\) for Space Optimization (storing one row).

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • DP on Grids: Fixed starting point, variable ending point (multi-target).

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Recursion Direction: Trying to start from the last row in recursion is harder because there are multiple starting points. Starting from the top (0, 0) is simpler.
  • Boundary Checks: Unlike rectangular grids, the number of columns changes per row in a triangle. Ensure loops for j only go up to i.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:

  • Unique Paths II
  • Falling Path Sum

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: Given \(N\) items with their weights wt and values val, and a bag of capacity W. You can pick each item an infinite number of times. Maximize the total value in the bag without exceeding capacity W.
  • Input: Array wt, Array val, integer W.
  • Output: Maximum total value.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This is exactly like 0/1 Knapsack, but with infinite supply.
  • Pick/Not-Pick:
  • Not Pick: Move to the previous item: f(index - 1, cap).
  • Pick: Stay at the same index and reduce capacity: val[index] + f(index, cap - wt[index]).
  • Base Case: At index 0, you can pick the item as many times as it fits: (capacity // wt[0]) * val[0].

docs.title: 1. Recursion with Memoization
docs.text:
• Code (Python): 
    def solve(index, cap, wt, val, dp):
    if index == 0:
        return (cap // wt[0]) * val[0]

    if dp[index][cap] != -1:
        return dp[index][cap]

    not_pick = solve(index - 1, cap, wt, val, dp)
    pick = -1e9
    if wt[index] <= cap:
        pick = val[index] + solve(index, cap - wt[index], wt, val, dp)

    dp[index][cap] = max(pick, not_pick)
    return dp[index][cap]

docs.title: 2. Tabulation
docs.text:
• Code (Python): 
    def unbounded_knapsack(n, w, wt, val):
    dp = [[0 for _ in range(w + 1)] for _ in range(n)]

    for cap in range(w + 1):
        dp[0][cap] = (cap // wt[0]) * val[0]

    for i in range(1, n):
        for cap in range(w + 1):
            not_pick = dp[i-1][cap]
            pick = -1e9
            if wt[i] <= cap:
                pick = val[i] + dp[i][cap - wt[i]]
            dp[i][cap] = max(pick, not_pick)

    return dp[n-1][w]

docs.title: 3. Space Optimization (Single Row)
docs.text:
• Explanation: To calculate dp[i][cap], we need dp[i-1][cap] (previous row) and dp[i][cap - wt[i]] (same row). By iterating forwards, we use the updated value of the current row, which naturally handles the infinite supply.
  • Code (Python): 
    def unbounded_knapsack(n, w, wt, val):
    prev = [0] * (w + 1)
    for cap in range(w + 1):
        prev[cap] = (cap // wt[0]) * val[0]

    for i in range(1, n):
        for cap in range(w + 1):
            not_pick = prev[cap]
            pick = -1e9
            if wt[i] <= cap:
                pick = val[i] + prev[cap - wt[i]]
            prev[cap] = max(pick, not_pick)

    return prev[w]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][cap] is the maximum value using items 0 to i with capacity cap.

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time Complexity: \(O(N \times W)\)
  • Space Complexity: \(O(W)\) with single row optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • Infinite Supply: Whenever you can reuse an item, the "pick" transition stays at the current index.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Iteration Order:
  • 0/1 Knapsack (Finite) -> Backwards iteration.
  • Unbounded Knapsack (Infinite) -> Forwards iteration.

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
• Problem: You are given an \(N \times M\) grid with some obstacles. You start at the top-left corner \((0, 0)\) and want to reach the bottom-right corner \((N-1, M-1)\). You can only move Down or Right. If a cell has an obstacle, you cannot pass through it.
  • Input: An \(N \times M\) matrix maze where maze[i][j] = -1 (or 1) represents an obstacle and 0 represents an empty path.
  • Output: Total number of unique paths to the destination.
  • Constraints: The answer should be modulo \(10^9 + 7\).

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
• This is a direct extension of the Grid Unique Paths problem.
  • The logic remains the same: the number of ways to reach \((i, j)\) is the sum of ways to reach \((i-1, j)\) and \((i, j-1)\).
  • Key Difference: If a cell \((i, j)\) is an obstacle, the number of ways to reach it is 0. This effectively stops any paths from passing through that cell.

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text:
• Explanation: Similar to Unique Paths I, but with an additional base case for obstacles.
  • Code (Python): 
    def count_paths(i, j, maze):
    if i >= 0 and j >= 0 and maze[i][j] == -1:
        return 0
    if i == 0 and j == 0:
        return 1
    if i < 0 or j < 0:
        return 0

    up = count_paths(i - 1, j, maze)
    left = count_paths(i, j - 1, maze)

    return up + left

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Cache the results of each cell to optimize the recursion.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve_memo(m, n, maze):
    dp = [[-1] * n for _ in range(m)]

    def solve(i, j):
        if i >= 0 and j >= 0 and maze[i][j] == -1:
            return 0
        if i == 0 and j == 0:
            return 1
        if i < 0 or j < 0:
            return 0
        if dp[i][j] != -1:
            return dp[i][j]

        dp[i][j] = (solve(i - 1, j) + solve(i, j - 1)) % (10**9 + 7)
        return dp[i][j]

    return solve(m - 1, n - 1)

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text:
• Explanation: Iteratively fill the DP table while checking for obstacles at each cell.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve_tab(m, n, maze):
    dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(m)]
    MOD = 10**9 + 7

    for i in range(m):
        for j in range(n):
            if maze[i][j] == -1:
                dp[i][j] = 0
            elif i == 0 and j == 0:
                dp[i][j] = 1
            else:
                up = dp[i-1][j] if i > 0 else 0
                left = dp[i][j-1] if j > 0 else 0
                dp[i][j] = (up + left) % MOD

    return dp[m-1][n-1]

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text:
• Explanation: Only the previous row is needed.
  • Code (Python): 
    def solve_opt(m, n, maze):
    prev = [0] * n
    MOD = 10**9 + 7
    for i in range(m):
        curr = [0] * n
        for j in range(n):
            if maze[i][j] == -1:
                curr[j] = 0
            elif i == 0 and j == 0:
                curr[j] = 1
            else:
                up = prev[j]
                left = curr[j-1] if j > 0 else 0
                curr[j] = (up + left) % MOD
        prev = curr
    return prev[n-1]

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: • dp[i][j] = Total number of unique paths from \((0, 0)\) to \((i, j)\) avoiding obstacles.

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
• If maze[i][j] == -1: \(f(i, j) = 0\)
  • Else: \(f(i, j) = f(i-1, j) + f(i, j-1)\)
  • Base Case: \(f(0, 0) = 1\) (if not an obstacle)

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N \times M)\)
  • Space: \(O(M)\) (Space Optimized) or \(O(N \times M)\) (Tabulation/Memoization)

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: • 2D DP: DP on Grids with Obstacles.

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Not checking if the start cell \((0, 0)\) or end cell \((N-1, M-1)\) is an obstacle.
  • Forgetting to apply modulo at each step.

docs.title: 🔗 Related Problems
docs.text:
• Grid Unique Paths
  • Minimum Path Sum

docs.title: 🧩 Problem Summary
docs.text:
Given an input string s2 (text) and a pattern s1 (wildcard), implement wildcard pattern matching with support for '?' and '*'.

  • '?' matches any single character.
  • '*' matches any sequence of characters (including the empty sequence).
  • The matching should cover the entire input string (not partial).

docs.title: Input/Output
docs.text:
• Input: s1 = "ab*cd", s2 = "abdefcd"
  • Output: true (since * matches "def")

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• s1 and s2 contain lowercase English letters.
  • s1 can contain '?' and '*'.

docs.title: 💡 Intuition
docs.text:
The problem is a variation of string matching. The challenge lies in the '*' character, which can match zero or more characters. This creates multiple branching paths: 1. Treat '*' as an empty string (skip it). 2. Treat '*' as matching one character and stay at the same pattern index to potentially match more.

Brute force (recursion) explores all these paths. Overlapping subproblems arise when different paths lead to the same state (i, j), where i is the index in the pattern and j is the index in the text.

docs.title: 1. Recursion
docs.text: Define f(i, j) as whether s1[0...i] matches s2[0...j]. - If s1[i] == s2[j] or s1[i] == '?', move to f(i-1, j-1). - If s1[i] == '*', two choices: - Match empty: f(i-1, j) - Match one or more: f(i, j-1) - Else: false.

docs.title: 2. Memoization (Top-Down DP)
docs.text: Use a 2D array dp[n][m] to store results of f(i, j) to avoid redundant calculations.

docs.title: 3. Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
docs.text: Convert the recursion to a 2D table. Handle base cases for i=0 and j=0 carefully. Note that i and j are 1-indexed in tabulation to handle empty string cases. - dp[0][0] = true (both empty match) - dp[0][j] = false (pattern empty, text not) - dp[i][0] = true only if all characters in s1[0...i-1] are '*'.

docs.title: 4. Space Optimization
docs.text: Since dp[i][j] only depends on the current row i and the previous row i-1, we can use two 1D arrays (prev and curr) of size m+1.

docs.title: 🧠 DP State Definition
docs.text: dp[i][j] represents whether the first i characters of the pattern match the first j characters of the text.

docs.title: 🔄 Recurrence Relation
docs.text:
if (s1[i-1] == s2[j-1] || s1[i-1] == '?')
    dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1];
else if (s1[i-1] == '*')
    dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] || dp[i][j-1];
else
    dp[i][j] = false;

docs.title: ⏱️ Complexity (Time & Space)
docs.text:
• Time: \(O(N \times M)\) where \(N\) and \(M\) are lengths of the strings.
  • Space: \(O(N \times M)\) for Tabulation, \(O(M)\) for Space Optimization.

docs.title: 📌 Pattern
docs.text: DP on Strings (Matching Pattern)

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Base Case for *: A pattern like *** matches an empty string. The base case must check if all remaining pattern characters are *.
  • String Indexing: Be careful with 1-based indexing in DP vs 0-based in strings.
